results.title: SeaTicket: Al agent that resolves issues across all your channels
results.content:
When presented with an issue, the AI agent will invoke the search tool multiple times with different queries until it believes it has gathered sufficient information to address the issue. Therefore, if the AI model is sufficiently powerful, it can reason about the answer even if one symptom can correspond to multiple potential root causes.

Gaurav Aroraa

Bringing full context into the resolution flow is the right call. Most support tools surface the ticket but not the relevant prior issues or docs. We've dealt with this fragmentation problem building customer-facing workflows. How does the context retrieval work under the hood? Is it vector search over your issue history, or do you use a graph structure to connect related threads?

Daniel Pan
SeaTable

SeaTable [...] Abdullah Bin Asad

the real question buried in these comments is the right one, suggesting a fix and autonomously closing tickets or messaging customers are very different risk levels. if the agent can act on github state or talk to customers directly, thats where you want an approval layer before confidence scores start making real decisions

Farrukh Butt

I like the idea that every resolved ticket makes the next one easier. Support history usually just sits there, so turning it into active context is a solid move. [...] Daniel Pan
SeaTable

SeaTable

@blah\_mad For changing state and assign labels, it can be done without approvals. For response, it is better for a manual review and approve.

Ahmad

@daniel\_pan1

Yep that makes sense. I agree responses should stay manual/reviewed for most teams.

For state changes and labels, I think the useful middle ground is auto-run with receipts. Even if no approval is needed, labels can change routing, priority, SLAs, downstream automations, and what the next human assumes happened.

So less “approve every label change” and more “prove later what changed, why, from which context, and who/what triggered it.” That is usually the part teams miss until support volume gets messy.

Daniel Pan
SeaTable

SeaTable

Hey Product Hunt! 👋

results.title: Krea Reviews (2026) | Product Hunt
results.content:
Howdy

#### What's great

Anna Sokolova 

#### What's great

I mostly use their Enhancer - it literally saves even the blurriest, softest source images. It doesn’t just bump up the resolution; it actually adds realistic texture - fabric starts looking like real fabric, skin like real skin. For prepping pics to post in high quality it’s a total must-have, especially when the original was generated at low res. And their pattern tool (Logo Illusions) works surprisingly cleanly too. I’ve tried embedding text and logos into natural textures and it blends super organically - no ugly seams or obvious edits

@tmorkes  @jared\_scheel  @harkirat\_singh3777 Thanks Harkirat. Early adoption usually comes down to two things:

1. Where the AI agent is placed in the user journey
2. Ticket volume

Teams with higher volume that surface the agent in a high-intent spot (help center, contact flow, billing pages) tend to see adoption almost immediately. That’s also why we’re comfortable backing the outcome with a guarantee.

Report

Anthony White [...] Report

Alex Turnbull

Groove

Groove

Maker

@saurabh\_ishere Thanks! Totally agree. Most tools just move work around. The hardest workflows so far haven’t been billing mechanics but intent-heavy edge cases. Things like refunds or plan changes where the user is frustrated, unclear, or mixing multiple requests in one message.

The automation itself is solvable. Getting intent right with zero blast radius is the real work, which is why Helply escalates early when signals aren’t clean.

Report

Harkirat Singh

Congrats on the launch @tmorkes @alexmturnbull @jared\_scheel !  
The guaranteed resolution rate is interesting. Curious how teams usually see adoption in the first few weeks.

#### What's great [...] Report

Alexey Glukharev

Congrats on the launch! 🎉 The pixel-art office framing is clever but there’s real substance underneath — making multi-agent collaboration visible and controllable is one of the harder UX problems in AI tooling right now. Most agent frameworks are basically black boxes. Building OceanMind, an AI-powered iOS breathwork app, as a small team, and the idea of watching different AI models plan, code, and review in a single observable flow is exactly the kind of transparency that actually builds trust in the output.

Question: how do you handle conflicts when agents disagree on approach — does the Team Leader have the final call or is there a resolution mechanism?

Report

results.title: Weavable: Give every AI agent persistent work context | Product Hunt
results.content:
On the tokens: pulling raw data into the context window costs you twice. Once on ingestion, then again on reasoning, while the LLM connects records, sorts by recency, and figures out what matters. Weavable's graph already knows the relationships and what changed when. The agent queries for the specific signals it needs, and the model only reasons over those.

The 90% is what we see on realistic workflows like pre-meeting briefs, renewal analysis, pipeline summaries, compared to the same thing built on raw MCP calls.

Grant MacLennan

@varunn very cool guys!

Arjun Reghu

@grantmac\_  @varunn So its basically RAG ??

Varun Nair
Weavable

Weavable [...] Varun Nair
Weavable

Weavable

@grantmac\_  @arjun\_reghu Not really. RAG retrieves chunks of unstructured text by semantic similarity and hands them to the model. We do the opposite: deterministic queries against a structured graph that already knows the relationships, the timeline, and what changed.

So the model isn't reasoning over a pile of similar-looking documents. It's reading a current, ranked view of work data with the connections resolved upstream. Different primitive, different failure modes, very different token profile.

Abesh Thakur
Weavable

Weavable

@grantmac\_ thank you for taking a look, and for the encouragement!

Ishan Roy [...] You're describing the iterative-retrieval loop: pull, reason, pull more, reason again. Each round trip costs latency and tokens, and with direct MCPs across multiple sources it compounds fast.

Weavable does that work upstream and continuously. By the time the agent queries, the connections are resolved, the signals are ranked, and the change history is already in the graph. The agent typically gets what it needs in one query against a context, not four against four data sources. Fewer round trips, lower latency, and roughly a tenth of the tokens.

In our testing we've not only found the responses to be of better quality but also found that the downstream model/agent becomes better at adhering to instructions too!

Fabian Exner
